name: Colosseum
summary: The Colosseum, also known as the Flavian Amphitheatre, is the largest ancient amphitheatre ever built. Constructed between 70-80 AD under the Flavian emperors, it could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial contests, public spectacles, and dramas. It remains an iconic symbol of Imperial Rome and is one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.

timeline.title: Construction Begins Under Vespasian
timeline.description: Emperor Vespasian of the Flavian dynasty orders the construction of the Colosseum on the site of Nero's former palace lake, the Domus Aurea. The project is funded by spoils from the Siege of Jerusalem in 70 AD.

timeline.title: Inauguration Under Titus
timeline.description: Emperor Titus inaugurates the Colosseum with 100 days of games including gladiatorial combats and wild animal fights. The amphitheatre seats approximately 50,000 to 80,000 spectators and features a complex system of underground tunnels called the hypogeum.

timeline.title: Fire Damage and Repair
timeline.description: A major fire damages the upper levels of the Colosseum, caused by lightning striking the wooden structures. Repairs take several years and the arena is not fully functional again until around 240 AD under Gordian III.

timeline.title: Last Recorded Games
timeline.description: The last known gladiatorial fights take place in the Colosseum. By this time, the Roman Empire has adopted Christianity and the violent spectacles have gradually fallen out of favor. The structure begins a slow decline.

timeline.title: Great Earthquake Collapse
timeline.description: A devastating earthquake strikes Rome, causing the entire south side of the Colosseum's outer wall to collapse. The fallen stone is subsequently quarried and reused in the construction of palaces, churches, and hospitals throughout Rome.

timeline.title: Papal Preservation Decree
timeline.description: Pope Benedict XIV declares the Colosseum a sacred site in honor of the Christian martyrs believed to have died there. He consecrates the building and prohibits further removal of stone, marking the beginning of formal preservation efforts.

timeline.title: Major Restoration Campaign
timeline.description: Italy launches a comprehensive restoration campaign to clean and stabilize the Colosseum after decades of pollution damage. Modern techniques remove centuries of grime and reinforce weakened structural elements, transforming the monument's appearance.

images.caption: The Colosseum at its inauguration under Emperor Titus, gleaming white with travertine limestone
The Colosseum in late antiquity as gladiatorial games ceased and nature began reclaiming the structure
The aftermath of the 1349 earthquake showing the collapsed southern exterior wall
The fully restored Colosseum illuminated at night, one of the most visited monuments in the world
